column2: the Pretty Okay
Blood of Philip the Pretty Okay
Philip of House Guldberg was no mighty conqueror, nor brilliant sage, but a man who was, by nearly all accounts, fairly decent at most things and embarrassingly confident about it.
Philip has once again managed to do something that was not quite legendary, yet somehow memorable enough that people keep mentioning it at supper.
A respectable performance.
I should probably boast about this.
The court had expected greatness. What they received was... acceptable.
This is mildly embarrassing.
They simply failed to understand my brilliance.
Against all expectation, Philip has stumbled into competence.
A triumph of accidental excellence.
Let us never speak of this again.
Tension in the Guldberg court has become impossible to ignore. Bjorn and Philip glare at one another with the intensity of men who would both prefer to be elsewhere.
This court is not big enough for both of us.
We shall keep this hatred polite.

Rumors spread through the court with suspicious efficiency, suggesting that Philip and Helena are altogether too comfortable in each other's company. No proof exists, but that has never stopped gossip.
Damn these whispers.
I can use this.
Kate has begun to show a remarkable talent for navigating the family’s chaos, whether by wisdom or by subtle manipulation remains uncertain.
She may be the smartest among us.
A little intrigue never hurt anyone.
Philip surveys his lands and, with only a moderate amount of evidence, concludes that he could probably be King of Denmark.
Honestly, yes. I probably could.
No, this level of greatness is enough.
